setTitle: AWS SAA Practice - IAM & EC2 Easy Set 2

questions.questionText: Which AWS service allows you to manage permissions for users, groups, and roles within your AWS account?

A.reason: A service that records and evaluates the configuration of AWS resources over time. It audits compliance but does not manage identities or permissions.

B.reason: A DDoS protection service for AWS applications. It has nothing to do with user permissions.

C.reason: Identity and Access Management is the central AWS service for creating and managing users, groups, roles, and policies that control access to AWS resources.

D.reason: A service for managing multiple AWS accounts under a single umbrella. While it supports Service Control Policies (SCPs), it is account-level governance, not the per-account user permission management service.

questions.Tips: The question asks which service *manages permissions for users, groups, and roles*. Config = auditing, Shield = DDoS, Organizations = multi-account governance. Only IAM manages identities and permissions.

questions.questionText: What is an EC2 Security Group?

options.A: An IAM policy that controls access to EC2 instances
options.B: A virtual firewall that controls inbound and outbound traffic to EC2 instances
options.C: A network ACL applied at the subnet level
options.D: An encryption key used to secure EC2 storage

A.description: An IAM policy that controls access to EC2 instances
A.reason: IAM policies control API-level access (who can call EC2 APIs like `StartInstances`). They do not control network traffic to instances.

B.description: A virtual firewall that controls inbound and outbound traffic to EC2 instances
B.reason: Security Groups act as stateful firewalls, allowing you to define rules for what traffic can reach (inbound) and leave (outbound) an EC2 instance.

C.description: A network ACL applied at the subnet level
C.reason: Network ACLs (NACLs) are stateless firewalls applied at the subnet level, not the instance level. Security Groups operate at the instance level and are stateful.

D.description: An encryption key used to secure EC2 storage
D.reason: Encryption for EC2 storage (EBS) is managed through AWS KMS keys, not Security Groups.

questions.Tips: A Security Group is a firewall, not an IAM policy or encryption tool. Eliminate IAM policy (wrong layer), encryption key (wrong service), and NACL (subnet-level, not instance-level). Security Groups are stateful, instance-level firewalls.

questions.questionText: In AWS IAM, what is the difference between authentication and authorization?

options.A: Authentication verifies identity; authorization determines what actions are permitted
options.B: Authorization verifies identity; authentication determines what actions are permitted
options.C: They both refer to the same concept in IAM
options.D: Authentication is for users; authorization is only for services

A.description: Authentication verifies identity; authorization determines what actions are permitted
A.reason: Authentication answers "Who are you?" (login credentials, MFA). Authorization answers "What are you allowed to do?" (IAM policies).

B.description: Authorization verifies identity; authentication determines what actions are permitted
B.reason: This reverses the definitions of authentication and authorization.

C.description: They both refer to the same concept in IAM
C.reason: Authentication and authorization are distinct steps in the access control process.

D.description: Authentication is for users; authorization is only for services
D.reason: Both humans (users) and services (roles) go through both authentication and authorization.

questions.Tips: Authentication = proving who you are (login). Authorization = what you can do (permissions). These are never the same thing. If you flip them, the logic of any security system breaks.

questions.questionText: Which EC2 pricing model is best for a workload that runs continuously for 3 years?

A.reason: Pay for compute capacity by the second with no long-term commitment. Best for short-term, unpredictable workloads.

B.reason: Spare capacity available at up to 90% discount. AWS can reclaim them at any time with a 2-minute notice, making them unsuitable for continuously running workloads that cannot tolerate interruption.

C.reason: Commitment to a specific instance type in exchange for a significant discount (up to 72% over On-Demand). The 3-year term offers the maximum discount for steady-state workloads.

D.reason: Physical servers dedicated exclusively to one customer, billed at On-Demand rates. Used for licensing or compliance requirements, not cost optimization.

questions.Tips: Continuous 3-year workload = maximum commitment = maximum discount. Spot = interruptible (eliminate). On-Demand = no commitment (eliminate). Dedicated Hosts = compliance tool (eliminate). 3-year RI wins.

questions.questionText: What does EC2 instance metadata allow you to do?

options.A: Encrypt EBS volumes attached to the instance
options.B: Retrieve information about the running instance from within the instance itself
options.C: Monitor the instance's CPU utilization in CloudWatch
options.D: Automatically scale the instance based on load

A.description: Encrypt EBS volumes attached to the instance
A.reason: EBS encryption is configured at volume creation time and managed through AWS KMS. Metadata has nothing to do with encryption.

B.description: Retrieve information about the running instance from within the instance itself
B.reason: EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) is accessible at `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` and provides details like instance ID, public IP, IAM role credentials, and more — all accessible from within the instance.

C.description: Monitor the instance's CPU utilization in CloudWatch
C.reason: CloudWatch collects and displays metrics. While the CloudWatch Agent can run on an instance, metadata itself does not push data to CloudWatch.

D.description: Automatically scale the instance based on load
D.reason: Auto Scaling is handled by Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling Groups, not instance metadata.

questions.Tips: Instance Metadata answers 'what am I?' from *inside* the instance. Eliminate CloudWatch (external monitoring), encryption (KMS), and Auto Scaling (capacity management). Metadata is an internal self-inspection service.

questions.questionText: An IAM Group is used to:

options.B: Organize EC2 instances into logical clusters
options.C: Manage permissions for a collection of IAM Users

A.reason: AWS services receive permissions through IAM Roles, not Groups.

B.description: Organize EC2 instances into logical clusters
B.reason: EC2 organization is done with placement groups, tags, or Auto Scaling Groups. IAM Groups are purely an identity construct.

C.description: Manage permissions for a collection of IAM Users
C.reason: An IAM Group lets you attach a policy once and have it apply to all users in the group, simplifying permission management for teams.

D.reason: Inter-VPC network control is done via VPC Peering, Transit Gateway, and Security Groups. IAM Groups have no networking role.

questions.Tips: Groups are for *human users*, not services or compute resources. Eliminate service permissions (Roles do that), EC2 organization (Tags/Placement Groups do that), and VPC traffic (Security Groups do that).

questions.questionText: Which EC2 storage type is ephemeral and loses its data when the instance is stopped or terminated?

A.reason: A durable, network-attached block storage volume. Data persists independently of the instance lifecycle — the volume continues to exist after the instance stops.

B.reason: A high-performance EBS volume type designed for I/O-intensive workloads. Like all EBS volumes, data is durable and persists beyond the instance lifecycle.

C.reason: Physically attached NVMe/SSD storage on the host machine running the instance. It provides very high I/O performance but all data is lost when the instance is stopped, hibernated, or terminated.

D.reason: A fully managed, scalable network file system that can be shared across many EC2 instances. Data is highly durable and persists regardless of any instance state.

questions.Tips: Instance Store = physically attached = ephemeral. Everything with 'EBS' in the name is durable network-attached storage. EFS is a managed shared file system. Only Instance Store loses data on stop/terminate.

questions.questionText: What is the principle of least privilege in the context of IAM?

options.A: Always grant administrator access to reduce the number of permission errors
options.B: Grant only the minimum permissions necessary to perform a task
options.C: Share credentials among multiple team members to improve collaboration
options.D: Allow all actions by default and restrict only sensitive operations

A.description: Always grant administrator access to reduce the number of permission errors
A.reason: Granting full admin access to avoid errors is a major security anti-pattern that dramatically increases the blast radius if an account is compromised.

B.description: Grant only the minimum permissions necessary to perform a task
B.reason: The principle of least privilege means giving each user, role, or service only the exact permissions it needs — no more. This limits the damage from compromised credentials or misconfigured services.

C.description: Share credentials among multiple team members to improve collaboration
C.reason: Sharing credentials undermines accountability and auditability. Each person or system should have their own identity.

D.description: Allow all actions by default and restrict only sensitive operations
D.reason: AWS already defaults to deny-all; this option describes the opposite of both AWS defaults and the least-privilege principle.

questions.Tips: Least privilege = minimum necessary permissions. Eliminate 'always grant admin' (opposite), 'share credentials' (security violation), and 'allow all by default' (opposite of least privilege).

questions.questionText: Which of the following actions can you perform using EC2 User Data?

options.A: Change the instance type after launch
options.B: Install software packages and run scripts at instance launch
options.C: Attach additional EBS volumes after the instance starts
options.D: Enable Multi-AZ replication for the instance

A.description: Change the instance type after launch
A.reason: Changing an instance type requires stopping the instance and modifying it through the console or CLI. User Data only runs at first boot and cannot alter the instance type.

B.description: Install software packages and run scripts at instance launch
B.reason: User Data scripts run as root/administrator during the first boot cycle. They are commonly used to install packages (`apt install`, `yum install`), configure services, and pull application code.

C.description: Attach additional EBS volumes after the instance starts
C.reason: EBS volumes are attached through the EC2 console, CLI, or API. User Data can run `mount` commands, but cannot itself provision and attach new volumes.

D.description: Enable Multi-AZ replication for the instance
D.reason: Multi-AZ replication is a concept for managed services like RDS and ElastiCache. Individual EC2 instances do not have a Multi-AZ replication toggle.

questions.Tips: User Data runs at first boot only. It cannot change instance type (API operation), attach EBS (management operation), or enable Multi-AZ (not an EC2 concept). It only runs shell/PowerShell scripts.

questions.questionText: Which IAM component is used to grant cross-account access to AWS resources?

options.C: IAM Role with a Trust Policy

A.reason: Used to organize IAM users within a single account and apply shared permissions. Groups have no cross-account capability.

B.reason: A set of rules governing password complexity and rotation for IAM Users. It has no relevance to cross-account access.

C.description: IAM Role with a Trust Policy
C.reason: A Trust Policy defines which principals (users, services, or accounts) are allowed to assume the role. By specifying another AWS account ID in the trust policy, you enable cross-account access through role assumption.

D.reason: Access keys are long-term credentials for a specific IAM User in one account. While they can be used programmatically from anywhere, sharing access keys across accounts is insecure and not the intended pattern.

questions.Tips: Cross-account = Role with Trust Policy. Groups are single-account. Password Policy manages passwords only. Access Keys are for programmatic access, not cross-account delegation. Trust Policies are the mechanism.
